tools.name: configuration_view
tools.description: Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML

minified.description: Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)

tools.name: events_list
tools.description: List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter events by field values (e.g. 'type=Warning', 'involvedObject.name=my-pod'). Supported fields: involvedObject.kind, involvedObject.name, involvedObject.namespace, involvedObject.uid, involvedObject.apiVersion, involvedObject.resourceVersion, involvedObject.fieldPath, reason, reportingComponent, source, type. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces

tools.name: namespaces_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter namespaces by field values (e.g. 'metadata.name=default', 'status.phase=Active'). Supported fields: metadata.name, status.phase. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

tools.name: nodes_log
tools.description: Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet

name.description: Name of the node to get logs from

query.description: query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")

tailLines.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)

tools.name: nodes_stats_summary
tools.description: Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics

name.description: Name of the node to get stats from

tools.name: nodes_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)

tools.name: pods_delete
tools.description: Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

name.description: Name of the Pod to delete

namespace.description: Namespace to delete the Pod from

tools.name: pods_exec
tools.description: Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command

command.description: Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]

container.description: Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod where the command will be executed

namespace.description: Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed

tools.name: pods_get
tools.description: Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod from

tools.name: pods_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_list_in_namespace
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_log
tools.description: Get the logs of a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

container.description: Name of the Pod container to get the logs from (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the logs from

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod logs from

tail.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, default: 100)

tools.name: pods_run
tools.description: Run a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided container image and optional name

image.description: Container Image to run in the Pod

name.description: Name of the Pod (Optional, random name if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to run the Pod in

port.description: TCP/IP port to expose from the Pod container (Optional, no port exposed if not provided)

tools.name: pods_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Pods in the all namespaces, the provided namespace, or the current namespace

all_namespaces.description: If true, list the resource consumption for all Pods in all namespaces. If false, list the resource consumption for Pods in the provided namespace or the current namespace

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Pods in the namespace if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pods resource consumption from (Optional, current namespace if not provided and all_namespaces is false)

tools.name: resources_create_or_update
tools.description:
Create or update a Kubernetes resource via Server-Side Apply. The manifest is the complete desired state: any field this tool previously set and the new manifest omits is removed. To edit an existing resource, fetch it with resources_get, modify it, then re-apply the full resource.
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

resource.description: Complete YAML or JSON representation of the Kubernetes resource (full desired state, not a partial patch). Include apiVersion, kind, metadata, and the full spec.

tools.name: resources_delete
tools.description:
Delete a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

gracePeriodSeconds.description: Optional duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Value must be non-negative integer. The value zero indicates delete immediately. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to delete the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will delete resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_get
tools.description:
Get a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_list
tools.description:
List Kubernetes resources and objects in the current cluster by providing their apiVersion and kind and optionally the namespace and label selector
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resources (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter resources by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'metadata.name=myresource'). Supported fields vary by resource type. For Pods: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

kind.description: kind of the resources (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the resources by label

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resources from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will list resources from all namespaces

tools.name: resources_scale
tools.description: Get or update the scale of a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, name, and optionally the namespace. If the scale is set in the tool call, the scale will be updated to that value. Always returns the current scale of the resource

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are apps/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: StatefulSet, Deployment)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to get/update the namespaced resource scale from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get/update resource scale from configured namespace

scale.description: Optional scale to update the resources scale to. If not provided, will return the current scale of the resource, and not update it

results.taskOutput:
Done. Here’s what I set up in canary-deployment-ns:

- Deployed canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (2 replicas) using image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Labels: app: recommendation-engine, version: v2.1
  - Container name: rec-engine
  - Container port: 80 (matching stable)
  - Security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext set)
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app: recommendation-engine (removed version label), so it targets both engine-v2-0 and engine-v2-1 pods.
  - Service remains port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Traffic split
- With 2 replicas in engine-v2-0 and 2 replicas in engine-v2-1, the Service will distribute roughly 50/50 across the 4 ready endpoints.

Verify
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get deploy engine-v2-1
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get endpoints recommendation-engine  # expect 4 addresses
- Optionally check logs/metrics to validate canary behavior.

Rollback/adjust
- To revert to stable only: update the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0.
- To adjust traffic, change replica counts proportionally between the two Deployments.

Done. Here’s what I set up in canary-deployment-ns:

- Deployed canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (2 replicas) using image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Labels: app: recommendation-engine, version: v2.1
  - Container name: rec-engine
  - Container port: 80 (matching stable)
  - Security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext set)
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app: recommendation-engine (removed version label), so it targets both engine-v2-0 and engine-v2-1 pods.
  - Service remains port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Traffic split
- With 2 replicas in engine-v2-0 and 2 replicas in engine-v2-1, the Service will distribute roughly 50/50 across the 4 ready endpoints.

Verify
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get deploy engine-v2-1
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get endpoints recommendation-engine  # expect 4 addresses
- Optionally check logs/metrics to validate canary behavior.

Rollback/adjust
- To revert to stable only: update the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0.
- To adjust traffic, change replica counts proportionally between the two Deployments.

outputSteps.content:
Done. Here’s what I set up in canary-deployment-ns:

- Deployed canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (2 replicas) using image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Labels: app: recommendation-engine, version: v2.1
  - Container name: rec-engine
  - Container port: 80 (matching stable)
  - Security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext set)
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app: recommendation-engine (removed version label), so it targets both engine-v2-0 and engine-v2-1 pods.
  - Service remains port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Traffic split
- With 2 replicas in engine-v2-0 and 2 replicas in engine-v2-1, the Service will distribute roughly 50/50 across the 4 ready endpoints.

Verify
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get deploy engine-v2-1
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get endpoints recommendation-engine  # expect 4 addresses
- Optionally check logs/metrics to validate canary behavior.

Rollback/adjust
- To revert to stable only: update the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0.
- To adjust traffic, change replica counts proportionally between the two Deployments.

Short answer: The Pod is healthy; your application inside it is throwing division-by-zero errors.

What I see:
- Pod status: Running, Ready, 0 restarts. No Kubernetes issues indicated.
- Recent app logs from calc-app-pod show intermittent failures:
  - “Run 4 failed with error: division by zero”
  - “Run 9 failed with error: division by zero”
  - “Run 12 failed with error: division by zero”
  - “Run 21 failed with error: division by zero”
- The container runs python /etc/config/calc-app.py from a ConfigMap (calc-app-map), so the bug is likely in that script or its inputs.

How to fix:
- Add a guard before any division (skip or default when denominator == 0).
- Wrap divisions in try/except ZeroDivisionError and log the offending inputs.
- Validate/sanitize any inputs or config values from the ConfigMap that can be zero.

If you want, I can open /etc/config/calc-app.py in the running Pod and point to the exact line causing the error.

- When you’re confident, we can scale down or remove the blue deployment.

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What was wrong
- The Deployment forced the container to run: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which doesn’t exist. That caused the pod to CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed
- Replaced the Deployment to remove the bad command/args.
- Kept the same labels and replicas.
- Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged
- Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent and exposed containerPort 8080 (the image listens on 8080 by default).

Current status
- Pod app-6bb64775d-9j428 in crashloop-test is Running (1/1), 0 restarts.
- Recent events show normal start-up with no further BackOffs.

If you want me to keep the prior Deployment history (no delete/recreate), I can instead patch the existing Deployment to explicitly clear command/args.

Steps.message:
Done.

What was wrong
- The Deployment forced the container to run: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which doesn’t exist. That caused the pod to CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed
- Replaced the Deployment to remove the bad command/args.
- Kept the same labels and replicas.
- Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged
- Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent and exposed containerPort 8080 (the image listens on 8080 by default).

Current status
- Pod app-6bb64775d-9j428 in crashloop-test is Running (1/1), 0 restarts.
- Recent events show normal start-up with no further BackOffs.

If you want me to keep the prior Deployment history (no delete/recreate), I can instead patch the existing Deployment to explicitly clear command/args.

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What was wrong
- The Deployment forced the container to run: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which doesn’t exist. That caused the pod to CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed
- Replaced the Deployment to remove the bad command/args.
- Kept the same labels and replicas.
- Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged
- Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent and exposed containerPort 8080 (the image listens on 8080 by default).

Current status
- Pod app-6bb64775d-9j428 in crashloop-test is Running (1/1), 0 restarts.
- Recent events show normal start-up with no further BackOffs.

If you want me to keep the prior Deployment history (no delete/recreate), I can instead patch the existing Deployment to explicitly clear command/args.

results.taskOutput:
I found the issue: the Deployment was using an invalid/private image tag, causing ImagePullBackOff.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment debug/app container image to a public, valid image: nginx:stable-alpine.

Current state
- Deployment debug/app is healthy.
- 1/1 replicas available and running (ReplicaSet app-5cf6588b85, Pod app-5cf6588b85-l9ls9).

If you prefer a specific nginx variant or registry, tell me and I’ll update the image accordingly.

outputSteps.content:
I found the issue: the Deployment was using an invalid/private image tag, causing ImagePullBackOff.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment debug/app container image to a public, valid image: nginx:stable-alpine.

Current state
- Deployment debug/app is healthy.
- 1/1 replicas available and running (ReplicaSet app-5cf6588b85, Pod app-5cf6588b85-l9ls9).

If you prefer a specific nginx variant or registry, tell me and I’ll update the image accordingly.

Steps.message:
deployment.apps/app condition met
Pod is stable. Verification successful.

results.taskOutput:
I fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod’s PVC homepage-pvc requested a non-existent StorageClass “sc”, so the claim stayed Pending and the pod couldn’t schedule. Events showed: storageclass.storage.k8s.io "sc" not found.

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (no data loss since it was never bound).
- Recreated the PVC with an existing StorageClass “standard” (provisioner rancher.io/local-path).

Current status
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi, RWO).
- Pod homepage-pod: Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.

Recommendation
- Update your manifests to use storageClassName: standard (or omit storageClassName to use the default) for homepage-pvc. If you need a different class, create it first, then reference it.

Steps.message:
namespace/homepage-ns created
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc created
pod/homepage-pod created

I fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod’s PVC homepage-pvc requested a non-existent StorageClass “sc”, so the claim stayed Pending and the pod couldn’t schedule. Events showed: storageclass.storage.k8s.io "sc" not found.

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (no data loss since it was never bound).
- Recreated the PVC with an existing StorageClass “standard” (provisioner rancher.io/local-path).

Current status
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi, RWO).
- Pod homepage-pod: Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.

Recommendation
- Update your manifests to use storageClassName: standard (or omit storageClassName to use the default) for homepage-pvc. If you need a different class, create it first, then reference it.

outputSteps.content:
I fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod’s PVC homepage-pvc requested a non-existent StorageClass “sc”, so the claim stayed Pending and the pod couldn’t schedule. Events showed: storageclass.storage.k8s.io "sc" not found.

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (no data loss since it was never bound).
- Recreated the PVC with an existing StorageClass “standard” (provisioner rancher.io/local-path).

Current status
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi, RWO).
- Pod homepage-pod: Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.

Recommendation
- Update your manifests to use storageClassName: standard (or omit storageClassName to use the default) for homepage-pvc. If you need a different class, create it first, then reference it.

Steps.message:
Starting verification for fix-pending-pods...
ℹWaiting for PVC 'homepage-pvc' to be 'Bound'...
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc condition met
'homepage-pvc' is Bound. Verifying that desired state is realized...
Waiting for Pod 'homepage-pod' to be 'Ready'...
pod/homepage-pod condition met

results.taskOutput:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged, which listens on port 8080 (not 80).
- Liveness/readiness probes were hitting port 80 on paths /get_status and /is_ready, so the container failed health checks and kept restarting.
- The Service targeted port 80, which didn’t match the container’s actual listen port.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to HTTP GET / on port 8080.
- Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080 (still serves on Service port 80 inside the cluster).

Current status
- Deployment webapp in namespace orders is healthy and Available.
- Pod is 1/1 Running and passing probes.
- Service orders/webapp is up on ClusterIP 10.96.211.214, port 80 -> 8080.

How to verify
- kubectl -n orders get deploy,po,svc
- Inside the cluster: curl http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local

Want me to expose it externally (Ingress/LoadBalancer) or adjust the probes if your app serves custom /is_ready and /get_status endpoints?

Steps.message:
namespace/orders created
deployment.apps/webapp created
service/webapp created
Waiting for pod to start and begin failing health checks...
deployment.apps/webapp condition met
Setup successful: Deployment is no longer available.

I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged, which listens on port 8080 (not 80).
- Liveness/readiness probes were hitting port 80 on paths /get_status and /is_ready, so the container failed health checks and kept restarting.
- The Service targeted port 80, which didn’t match the container’s actual listen port.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to HTTP GET / on port 8080.
- Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080 (still serves on Service port 80 inside the cluster).

Current status
- Deployment webapp in namespace orders is healthy and Available.
- Pod is 1/1 Running and passing probes.
- Service orders/webapp is up on ClusterIP 10.96.211.214, port 80 -> 8080.

How to verify
- kubectl -n orders get deploy,po,svc
- Inside the cluster: curl http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local

Want me to expose it externally (Ingress/LoadBalancer) or adjust the probes if your app serves custom /is_ready and /get_status endpoints?

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
          protocol: TCP
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
            scheme: HTTP
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          successThreshold: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
            scheme: HTTP
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          successThreshold: 1
          failureThreshold: 3

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
  labels:
    app: webapp
spec:
  selector:
    app: webapp
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
    protocol: TCP
  type: ClusterIP

        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
          protocol: TCP
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
            scheme: HTTP
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          successThreshold: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
            scheme: HTTP
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          successThreshold: 1
          failureThreshold: 3

    protocol: TCP

outputSteps.content:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged, which listens on port 8080 (not 80).
- Liveness/readiness probes were hitting port 80 on paths /get_status and /is_ready, so the container failed health checks and kept restarting.
- The Service targeted port 80, which didn’t match the container’s actual listen port.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to HTTP GET / on port 8080.
- Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080 (still serves on Service port 80 inside the cluster).

Current status
- Deployment webapp in namespace orders is healthy and Available.
- Pod is 1/1 Running and passing probes.
- Service orders/webapp is up on ClusterIP 10.96.211.214, port 80 -> 8080.

How to verify
- kubectl -n orders get deploy,po,svc
- Inside the cluster: curl http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local

Want me to expose it externally (Ingress/LoadBalancer) or adjust the probes if your app serves custom /is_ready and /get_status endpoints?

Steps.message:
Checking if the pod is running and ready...
pod/webapp-76f8fd8b9f-bd7fx condition met
Success: Pod is now Ready
Current liveness probe path: /
Current readiness probe path: /
Success: Both probe paths have been fixed
Success: Pod is stable with acceptable number of restarts

results.taskOutput:
Root cause
- The Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant any permissions on core/v1 pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount couldn’t list pods.

What I fixed
- Updated the Role to include permissions on pods:
  - apiGroup: "" (core), resources: ["pods"], verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
  - Kept the existing apps/deployments list rule.

Your setup now
- ServiceAccount: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader
- Role: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role (now includes pods get/list/watch)
- RoleBinding: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-binding binds the SA to the Role

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods -n simple-rbac-setup
- kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader get pods -n simple-rbac-setup

Notes
- If you try to list pods across all namespaces (-A), you’ll need a ClusterRole with a ClusterRoleBinding instead of a namespaced Role.

arguments.resource:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader-role
  namespace: simple-rbac-setup
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["list"]

Steps.message:
namespace/simple-rbac-setup created
serviceaccount/pod-reader created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-role created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-binding created

Root cause
- The Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant any permissions on core/v1 pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount couldn’t list pods.

What I fixed
- Updated the Role to include permissions on pods:
  - apiGroup: "" (core), resources: ["pods"], verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
  - Kept the existing apps/deployments list rule.

Your setup now
- ServiceAccount: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader
- Role: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role (now includes pods get/list/watch)
- RoleBinding: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-binding binds the SA to the Role

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods -n simple-rbac-setup
- kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader get pods -n simple-rbac-setup

Notes
- If you try to list pods across all namespaces (-A), you’ll need a ClusterRole with a ClusterRoleBinding instead of a namespaced Role.

outputSteps.content:
Root cause
- The Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant any permissions on core/v1 pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount couldn’t list pods.

What I fixed
- Updated the Role to include permissions on pods:
  - apiGroup: "" (core), resources: ["pods"], verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
  - Kept the existing apps/deployments list rule.

Your setup now
- ServiceAccount: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader
- Role: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role (now includes pods get/list/watch)
- RoleBinding: simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-binding binds the SA to the Role

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods -n simple-rbac-setup
- kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader get pods -n simple-rbac-setup

Notes
- If you try to list pods across all namespaces (-A), you’ll need a ClusterRole with a ClusterRoleBinding instead of a namespaced Role.

Steps.message: Verification successful: RBAC role correctly reconfigured.

results.taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- Service selector mismatch: Service nginx selected pods with label app=web, but your Deployment labels pods app=nginx. That left the Service with no endpoints.
- Port mismatch: The container (nginx-unprivileged) listens on 8080, while the Service targetPort was 80.

What I changed (fixed)
- Updated Service web/nginx to:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: port 80 -> targetPort 8080
  - Kept the existing clusterIP unchanged

Verification
- Endpoints now back the Service:
  - web/nginx -> 10.244.0.34:8080
- In-cluster access works:
  - From a test pod, curl http://nginx:80 returned the standard “Welcome to nginx!” page.

Useful checks
- kubectl -n web get svc nginx -o wide
- kubectl -n web get endpoints nginx -o yaml
- kubectl -n web run -it --rm nettest --image=wbitt/network-multitool:alpine-minimal -- wget -qO- http://nginx:80

If you need external access
Pick one of the following:
- Port-forward (quick/local):
  - kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80
  - Then browse http://localhost:8080
- NodePort (cluster nodes’ IPs):
  - kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort","ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8080,"protocol":"TCP","nodePort":30080}]}}'
  - Then browse http://<node-ip>:30080
- Ingress (preferred behind an Ingress controller):
  - Create an Ingress mapping host/path to service nginx:80.

Notes
- I left a small test pod (nettool) in namespace web that successfully validated the Service. Say the word if you want me to remove it.

Welcome to nginx!

If you see this page, nginx is successfully installed and working. Further configuration is required for the web server, reverse proxy, API gateway, load balancer, content cache, or other features.

For online documentation and support please refer to nginx.org.
To engage with the community please visit community.nginx.org.
For enterprise grade support, professional services, additional security features and capabilities please refer to f5.com/nginx.

Thank you for using nginx.

command: wget -qSO- http://nginx:80/ -T 2 || nc -vz nginx 80 || getent hosts nginx
arguments.name: nettool

Steps.message:
namespace/web created
deployment.apps/nginx created
service/nginx created

Diagnosis
- Service selector mismatch: Service nginx selected pods with label app=web, but your Deployment labels pods app=nginx. That left the Service with no endpoints.
- Port mismatch: The container (nginx-unprivileged) listens on 8080, while the Service targetPort was 80.

What I changed (fixed)
- Updated Service web/nginx to:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: port 80 -> targetPort 8080
  - Kept the existing clusterIP unchanged

Verification
- Endpoints now back the Service:
  - web/nginx -> 10.244.0.34:8080
- In-cluster access works:
  - From a test pod, curl http://nginx:80 returned the standard “Welcome to nginx!” page.

Useful checks
- kubectl -n web get svc nginx -o wide
- kubectl -n web get endpoints nginx -o yaml
- kubectl -n web run -it --rm nettest --image=wbitt/network-multitool:alpine-minimal -- wget -qO- http://nginx:80

If you need external access
Pick one of the following:
- Port-forward (quick/local):
  - kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80
  - Then browse http://localhost:8080
- NodePort (cluster nodes’ IPs):
  - kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort","ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8080,"protocol":"TCP","nodePort":30080}]}}'
  - Then browse http://<node-ip>:30080
- Ingress (preferred behind an Ingress controller):
  - Create an Ingress mapping host/path to service nginx:80.

Notes
- I left a small test pod (nettool) in namespace web that successfully validated the Service. Say the word if you want me to remove it.

outputSteps.content:
Diagnosis
- Service selector mismatch: Service nginx selected pods with label app=web, but your Deployment labels pods app=nginx. That left the Service with no endpoints.
- Port mismatch: The container (nginx-unprivileged) listens on 8080, while the Service targetPort was 80.

What I changed (fixed)
- Updated Service web/nginx to:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: port 80 -> targetPort 8080
  - Kept the existing clusterIP unchanged

Verification
- Endpoints now back the Service:
  - web/nginx -> 10.244.0.34:8080
- In-cluster access works:
  - From a test pod, curl http://nginx:80 returned the standard “Welcome to nginx!” page.

Useful checks
- kubectl -n web get svc nginx -o wide
- kubectl -n web get endpoints nginx -o yaml
- kubectl -n web run -it --rm nettest --image=wbitt/network-multitool:alpine-minimal -- wget -qO- http://nginx:80

If you need external access
Pick one of the following:
- Port-forward (quick/local):
  - kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80
  - Then browse http://localhost:8080
- NodePort (cluster nodes’ IPs):
  - kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort","ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8080,"protocol":"TCP","nodePort":30080}]}}'
  - Then browse http://<node-ip>:30080
- Ingress (preferred behind an Ingress controller):
  - Create an Ingress mapping host/path to service nginx:80.

Notes
- I left a small test pod (nettool) in namespace web that successfully validated the Service. Say the word if you want me to remove it.

pod "test-connection" deleted from web namespace

results.taskOutput:
Here’s what I found and fixed.

Root causes
1) Pods could not be scheduled
- The Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu.
- Your only node doesn’t have that label, so the scheduler kept the pods Pending.
- Evidence: Events showed “0/1 nodes are available: didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector”.

2) Pods were starting but not becoming Ready (connection refused)
- After scheduling, the containers ran but readiness/liveness probes to port 80 failed with “connection refused”.
- The image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged does not listen on privileged ports (<1024) by default; it serves on 8080.
- So the probes and Service pointing to port 80 couldn’t reach the app.

What I changed
- Removed the blocking nodeSelector by recreating the Deployment without it (so the pods can schedule on the existing node).
- Aligned ports with the unprivileged NGINX image:
  - Set containerPort to 8080.
  - Updated readiness and liveness probes to HTTP GET on port 8080.
  - Updated the Service web-app-service to targetPort: 8080 (Service port remains 80).
- Pinned the image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.28 for reproducibility.

Current status
- Pods are now running and Ready:
  - web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-6t9sp 1/1 Running
  - web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-fqtfx 1/1 Running
- Service endpoints are healthy and point to the pods on port 8080.
- The ClusterIP Service still exposes port 80, so your frontend can keep calling http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local:80.

How to verify
- From inside the cluster:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run -it --rm tmp --image=wbitt/network-multitool:alpine-minimal -- curl -sI web-app-service:80
  - Expect “HTTP/1.1 200 OK”.

Notes/next steps (if desired)
- If you do need to constrain to specific nodes (e.g., GPU), either:
  - Label the appropriate nodes with environment=production-gpu, or
  - Reintroduce nodeSelector/affinity once matching nodes are available.
- If you prefer to serve on port 80 inside the container, use a rootful NGINX image or add a capability to bind to privileged ports; otherwise keep using 8080 in-container with a Service on 80.

Let me know if you want me to reapply a node constraint with correct node labels, or adjust probes/resources further.

NAMESPACE          APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   IP            NODE                             NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS
webshop-frontend   v1           Pod    web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-6t9sp   1/1     Running   0          14s   10.244.0.40   mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane   <none>           <none>            app=web-app,pod-template-hash=577d4659dc
webshop-frontend   v1           Pod    web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-fqtfx   1/1     Running   0          9s    10.244.0.41   mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane   <none>           <none>            app=web-app,pod-template-hash=577d4659dc

Steps.message:
namespace/webshop-frontend created
service/web-app-service created
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment created
Waiting for resources to be created...
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment condition met
Warning: v1 Endpoints is deprecated in v1.33+; use discovery.k8s.io/v1 EndpointSlice
Setup successful: Service has no endpoints as expected

Here’s what I found and fixed.

Root causes
1) Pods could not be scheduled
- The Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu.
- Your only node doesn’t have that label, so the scheduler kept the pods Pending.
- Evidence: Events showed “0/1 nodes are available: didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector”.

2) Pods were starting but not becoming Ready (connection refused)
- After scheduling, the containers ran but readiness/liveness probes to port 80 failed with “connection refused”.
- The image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged does not listen on privileged ports (<1024) by default; it serves on 8080.
- So the probes and Service pointing to port 80 couldn’t reach the app.

What I changed
- Removed the blocking nodeSelector by recreating the Deployment without it (so the pods can schedule on the existing node).
- Aligned ports with the unprivileged NGINX image:
  - Set containerPort to 8080.
  - Updated readiness and liveness probes to HTTP GET on port 8080.
  - Updated the Service web-app-service to targetPort: 8080 (Service port remains 80).
- Pinned the image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.28 for reproducibility.

Current status
- Pods are now running and Ready:
  - web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-6t9sp 1/1 Running
  - web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-fqtfx 1/1 Running
- Service endpoints are healthy and point to the pods on port 8080.
- The ClusterIP Service still exposes port 80, so your frontend can keep calling http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local:80.

How to verify
- From inside the cluster:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run -it --rm tmp --image=wbitt/network-multitool:alpine-minimal -- curl -sI web-app-service:80
  - Expect “HTTP/1.1 200 OK”.

Notes/next steps (if desired)
- If you do need to constrain to specific nodes (e.g., GPU), either:
  - Label the appropriate nodes with environment=production-gpu, or
  - Reintroduce nodeSelector/affinity once matching nodes are available.
- If you prefer to serve on port 80 inside the container, use a rootful NGINX image or add a capability to bind to privileged ports; otherwise keep using 8080 in-container with a Service on 80.

Let me know if you want me to reapply a node constraint with correct node labels, or adjust probes/resources further.

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment

  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 25%
      maxUnavailable: 25%
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          protocol: TCP
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 25%
      maxUnavailable: 25%
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.28
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          protocol: TCP
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.28
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        ports:
        - name: http
          containerPort: 8080
          protocol: TCP
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        resources: {}
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 25%
      maxUnavailable: 25%
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          protocol: TCP
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 25%
      maxUnavailable: 25%
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.28
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          protocol: TCP
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.28
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        ports:
        - name: http
          containerPort: 8080
          protocol: TCP
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        resources: {}
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30

outputSteps.content:
Here’s what I found and fixed.

Root causes
1) Pods could not be scheduled
- The Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu.
- Your only node doesn’t have that label, so the scheduler kept the pods Pending.
- Evidence: Events showed “0/1 nodes are available: didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector”.

2) Pods were starting but not becoming Ready (connection refused)
- After scheduling, the containers ran but readiness/liveness probes to port 80 failed with “connection refused”.
- The image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged does not listen on privileged ports (<1024) by default; it serves on 8080.
- So the probes and Service pointing to port 80 couldn’t reach the app.

What I changed
- Removed the blocking nodeSelector by recreating the Deployment without it (so the pods can schedule on the existing node).
- Aligned ports with the unprivileged NGINX image:
  - Set containerPort to 8080.
  - Updated readiness and liveness probes to HTTP GET on port 8080.
  - Updated the Service web-app-service to targetPort: 8080 (Service port remains 80).
- Pinned the image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.28 for reproducibility.

Current status
- Pods are now running and Ready:
  - web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-6t9sp 1/1 Running
  - web-app-deployment-577d4659dc-fqtfx 1/1 Running
- Service endpoints are healthy and point to the pods on port 8080.
- The ClusterIP Service still exposes port 80, so your frontend can keep calling http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local:80.

How to verify
- From inside the cluster:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run -it --rm tmp --image=wbitt/network-multitool:alpine-minimal -- curl -sI web-app-service:80
  - Expect “HTTP/1.1 200 OK”.

Notes/next steps (if desired)
- If you do need to constrain to specific nodes (e.g., GPU), either:
  - Label the appropriate nodes with environment=production-gpu, or
  - Reintroduce nodeSelector/affinity once matching nodes are available.
- If you prefer to serve on port 80 inside the container, use a rootful NGINX image or add a capability to bind to privileged ports; otherwise keep using 8080 in-container with a Service on 80.

Let me know if you want me to reapply a node constraint with correct node labels, or adjust probes/resources further.

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I created
- Namespace: multi-container-logging (created because it didn’t exist)
- Pod: communication-pod in multi-container-logging with:
  - Container web-server: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    - Exposes port 8080
    - Readiness probe: HTTP GET / on port 8080 (pod ready when web server serves traffic)
    - Mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
  - Container logger: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    - Command: /bin/sh -c "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"
    - Mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx (readOnly)
  - Volume: emptyDir named logs-volume, mounted at /var/log/nginx in both containers
  - Pod securityContext fsGroup: 101 to allow nginx to write logs to the shared volume

Status
- Pod is Running and Ready (ContainersReady=True, Ready=True).

How to verify
- Check pod status:
  kubectl -n multi-container-logging get pod communication-pod -o wide
- Watch access logs from the logger container:
  kubectl -n multi-container-logging logs -f pod/communication-pod -c logger
- Test web server readiness from your workstation:
  kubectl -n multi-container-logging port-forward pod/communication-pod 8080:8080
  curl -I http://127.0.0.1:8080/
You should see log lines appear in the logger container as you make requests.

    - Exposes port 8080
    - Readiness probe: HTTP GET / on port 8080 (pod ready when web server serves traffic)
    - Mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
  - Container logger: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    - Command: /bin/sh -c "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"
    - Mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx (readOnly)
  - Volume: emptyDir named logs-volume, mounted at /var/log/nginx in both containers
  - Pod securityContext fsGroup: 101 to allow nginx to write logs to the shared volume

Status
- Pod is Running and Ready (ContainersReady=True, Ready=True).

How to verify
- Check pod status:
  kubectl -n multi-container-logging get pod communication-pod -o wide
- Watch access logs from the logger container:
  kubectl -n multi-container-logging logs -f pod/communication-pod -c logger
- Test web server readiness from your workstation:
  kubectl -n multi-container-logging port-forward pod/communication-pod 8080:8080
  curl -I http://127.0.0.1:8080/
You should see log lines appear in the logger container as you make requests.

results.taskOutput:
I updated the PVC used by storage-pod to request 3Gi and enabled expansion on the standard StorageClass.

Summary:
- PVC resize: storage-pvc in resize-pv now requests 3Gi.
- StorageClass: standard updated with allowVolumeExpansion: true.

Note:
- The current provisioner rancher.io/local-path does not support actual expansion via an external resizer. As a result, the PVC status still shows capacity 1Gi and the volume won’t be “expanded” by Kubernetes. Since it’s a hostPath-backed directory, there’s no enforced quota and the pod can still use the node’s available disk space, but you won’t see 3Gi reflected in PVC/PV capacity.

If you need a true 3Gi volume (with capacity reflected and enforceable), I can:
- Migrate the data to a new PVC on a CSI driver that supports expansion, or
- Re-provision a new PVC at 3Gi with a suitable StorageClass and move the pod to it.

Tell me which approach you prefer.

Steps.message:
namespace/resize-pv created
persistentvolumeclaim/storage-pvc created
pod/storage-pod created

I updated the PVC used by storage-pod to request 3Gi and enabled expansion on the standard StorageClass.

Summary:
- PVC resize: storage-pvc in resize-pv now requests 3Gi.
- StorageClass: standard updated with allowVolumeExpansion: true.

Note:
- The current provisioner rancher.io/local-path does not support actual expansion via an external resizer. As a result, the PVC status still shows capacity 1Gi and the volume won’t be “expanded” by Kubernetes. Since it’s a hostPath-backed directory, there’s no enforced quota and the pod can still use the node’s available disk space, but you won’t see 3Gi reflected in PVC/PV capacity.

If you need a true 3Gi volume (with capacity reflected and enforceable), I can:
- Migrate the data to a new PVC on a CSI driver that supports expansion, or
- Re-provision a new PVC at 3Gi with a suitable StorageClass and move the pod to it.

Tell me which approach you prefer.

outputSteps.content:
I updated the PVC used by storage-pod to request 3Gi and enabled expansion on the standard StorageClass.

Summary:
- PVC resize: storage-pvc in resize-pv now requests 3Gi.
- StorageClass: standard updated with allowVolumeExpansion: true.

Note:
- The current provisioner rancher.io/local-path does not support actual expansion via an external resizer. As a result, the PVC status still shows capacity 1Gi and the volume won’t be “expanded” by Kubernetes. Since it’s a hostPath-backed directory, there’s no enforced quota and the pod can still use the node’s available disk space, but you won’t see 3Gi reflected in PVC/PV capacity.

If you need a true 3Gi volume (with capacity reflected and enforceable), I can:
- Migrate the data to a new PVC on a CSI driver that supports expansion, or
- Re-provision a new PVC at 3Gi with a suitable StorageClass and move the pod to it.

Tell me which approach you prefer.

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I changed
- Updated Deployment rollout-test/web-app container image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.25.
- Tightened rolling update strategy to ensure zero downtime:
  - maxUnavailable: 0
  - maxSurge: 1

Verification
- Rollout completed successfully: replicas=3, updatedReplicas=3, availableReplicas=3, observedGeneration=3.
- Events show new pods were brought up before old ones were terminated (scaled up to 4 temporarily, then scaled down), indicating no loss of availability.

Notes to further harden zero-downtime
- The Deployment currently has no readinessProbe. For strict zero-downtime under load, add a readinessProbe so pods only receive traffic when truly ready. I can add a simple HTTP GET probe if you’d like.
- Optionally set minReadySeconds (e.g., 5–10s) and a preStop hook for graceful termination.

Rollback (if needed)
- kubectl -n rollout-test rollout undo deployment/web-app

Steps.message:
namespace/rollout-test created
deployment.apps/web-app created
deployment.apps/web-app condition met
Setup succeeded for rolling-update-deployment

Done.

What I changed
- Updated Deployment rollout-test/web-app container image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.25.
- Tightened rolling update strategy to ensure zero downtime:
  - maxUnavailable: 0
  - maxSurge: 1

Verification
- Rollout completed successfully: replicas=3, updatedReplicas=3, availableReplicas=3, observedGeneration=3.
- Events show new pods were brought up before old ones were terminated (scaled up to 4 temporarily, then scaled down), indicating no loss of availability.

Notes to further harden zero-downtime
- The Deployment currently has no readinessProbe. For strict zero-downtime under load, add a readinessProbe so pods only receive traffic when truly ready. I can add a simple HTTP GET probe if you’d like.
- Optionally set minReadySeconds (e.g., 5–10s) and a preStop hook for graceful termination.

Rollback (if needed)
- kubectl -n rollout-test rollout undo deployment/web-app

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I changed
- Updated Deployment rollout-test/web-app container image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.25.
- Tightened rolling update strategy to ensure zero downtime:
  - maxUnavailable: 0
  - maxSurge: 1

Verification
- Rollout completed successfully: replicas=3, updatedReplicas=3, availableReplicas=3, observedGeneration=3.
- Events show new pods were brought up before old ones were terminated (scaled up to 4 temporarily, then scaled down), indicating no loss of availability.

Notes to further harden zero-downtime
- The Deployment currently has no readinessProbe. For strict zero-downtime under load, add a readinessProbe so pods only receive traffic when truly ready. I can add a simple HTTP GET probe if you’d like.
- Optionally set minReadySeconds (e.g., 5–10s) and a preStop hook for graceful termination.

Rollback (if needed)
- kubectl -n rollout-test rollout undo deployment/web-app

results.taskOutput:
All requested configurations have been created and applied to the cluster. Summary of what’s in place:

Namespaces
- Developer namespaces: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
- Shared/dev environment: dev-shared
- Environments: staging, prod
- System namespaces (kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease) left unchanged

RBAC
- ServiceAccounts:
  - dev-alice/alice-sa
  - dev-bob/bob-sa
  - dev-charlie/charlie-sa
- Per-developer full access to their own namespace:
  - Role namespace-admin (full rights within namespace) in each of dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
  - RoleBindings:
    - dev-alice/alice-full-access -> subjects: user alice and SA alice-sa
    - dev-bob/bob-full-access -> subjects: user bob and SA bob-sa
    - dev-charlie/charlie-full-access -> subjects: user charlie and SA charlie-sa
- Read-only access to dev-shared for all developers and their SAs:
  - Role dev-shared/shared-readonly with verbs: get, list, watch
  - RoleBinding dev-shared/developers-readonly -> subjects: users alice, bob, charlie and SAs alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa
- Staging/prod: no developer bindings created. Only users with cluster-admin (e.g., system:masters) can access.

Resource Quotas
- dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie (quota-standard):
  - requests.cpu: 2, limits.cpu: 2
  - requests.memory: 4Gi, limits.memory: 4Gi
  - pods: 10, services: 5
- dev-shared (quota-shared):
  - requests.cpu: 4, limits.cpu: 4
  - requests.memory: 8Gi, limits.memory: 8Gi
  - pods: 20, services: 10
- staging, prod (quota-env):
  - requests.cpu: 8, limits.cpu: 8
  - requests.memory: 16Gi, limits.memory: 16Gi
  - pods: 50, services: 20

Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress and Egress) applied to: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Same-namespace allow (Ingress and Egress) in each namespace above to permit intra-namespace communication
- Allow egress to kube-system in all non-system namespaces (to enable DNS and system service access)
- Developer egress scoped:
  - dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie: allow egress to dev-shared
- dev-shared ingress from dev namespaces only:
  - Allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces using namespaceSelector matching env=dev and purpose=developer
- Cross-developer isolation:
  - No policies allow cross-dev namespace communications; default deny plus lack of ingress rules enforce this
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - Only same-namespace traffic allowed; no ingress from dev namespaces; egress only to kube-system for DNS

Notes and validation
- Ensure your authentication setup maps users alice, bob, charlie as Kubernetes users (e.g., via client cert CNs or OIDC). The RBAC above expects those identities.
- Quick validation commands:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'            -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods         -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared create pods      -> no
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods            -> no
  - kubectl -n dev-alice run -it tmp --image=busybox -- rm -rf /          -> should schedule but be constrained by quota
  - From a pod in dev-alice, curl a service in dev-bob                    -> blocked
  - From a pod in dev-alice, nslookup kubernetes.default.svc              -> succeeds (DNS via kube-system)
  - From a pod in dev-alice, reach a service in dev-shared                -> allowed
  - From a pod in dev-alice, reach a service in staging/prod              -> blocked

If you want explicit RoleBindings in staging/prod for your admin group, bind ClusterRole cluster-admin to your admin group (e.g., system:masters) in those namespaces, although it’s typically unnecessary since cluster-admin is cluster-scoped.

Steps.message:
Setting up cluster state for dev environment eval...
Setup completed successfully

All requested configurations have been created and applied to the cluster. Summary of what’s in place:

Namespaces
- Developer namespaces: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
- Shared/dev environment: dev-shared
- Environments: staging, prod
- System namespaces (kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease) left unchanged

RBAC
- ServiceAccounts:
  - dev-alice/alice-sa
  - dev-bob/bob-sa
  - dev-charlie/charlie-sa
- Per-developer full access to their own namespace:
  - Role namespace-admin (full rights within namespace) in each of dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
  - RoleBindings:
    - dev-alice/alice-full-access -> subjects: user alice and SA alice-sa
    - dev-bob/bob-full-access -> subjects: user bob and SA bob-sa
    - dev-charlie/charlie-full-access -> subjects: user charlie and SA charlie-sa
- Read-only access to dev-shared for all developers and their SAs:
  - Role dev-shared/shared-readonly with verbs: get, list, watch
  - RoleBinding dev-shared/developers-readonly -> subjects: users alice, bob, charlie and SAs alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa
- Staging/prod: no developer bindings created. Only users with cluster-admin (e.g., system:masters) can access.

Resource Quotas
- dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie (quota-standard):
  - requests.cpu: 2, limits.cpu: 2
  - requests.memory: 4Gi, limits.memory: 4Gi
  - pods: 10, services: 5
- dev-shared (quota-shared):
  - requests.cpu: 4, limits.cpu: 4
  - requests.memory: 8Gi, limits.memory: 8Gi
  - pods: 20, services: 10
- staging, prod (quota-env):
  - requests.cpu: 8, limits.cpu: 8
  - requests.memory: 16Gi, limits.memory: 16Gi
  - pods: 50, services: 20

Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress and Egress) applied to: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Same-namespace allow (Ingress and Egress) in each namespace above to permit intra-namespace communication
- Allow egress to kube-system in all non-system namespaces (to enable DNS and system service access)
- Developer egress scoped:
  - dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie: allow egress to dev-shared
- dev-shared ingress from dev namespaces only:
  - Allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces using namespaceSelector matching env=dev and purpose=developer
- Cross-developer isolation:
  - No policies allow cross-dev namespace communications; default deny plus lack of ingress rules enforce this
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - Only same-namespace traffic allowed; no ingress from dev namespaces; egress only to kube-system for DNS

Notes and validation
- Ensure your authentication setup maps users alice, bob, charlie as Kubernetes users (e.g., via client cert CNs or OIDC). The RBAC above expects those identities.
- Quick validation commands:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'            -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods         -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared create pods      -> no
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods            -> no
  - kubectl -n dev-alice run -it tmp --image=busybox -- rm -rf /          -> should schedule but be constrained by quota
  - From a pod in dev-alice, curl a service in dev-bob                    -> blocked
  - From a pod in dev-alice, nslookup kubernetes.default.svc              -> succeeds (DNS via kube-system)
  - From a pod in dev-alice, reach a service in dev-shared                -> allowed
  - From a pod in dev-alice, reach a service in staging/prod              -> blocked

If you want explicit RoleBindings in staging/prod for your admin group, bind ClusterRole cluster-admin to your admin group (e.g., system:masters) in those namespaces, although it’s typically unnecessary since cluster-admin is cluster-scoped.

  - dev-charlie/charlie-sa
- Per-developer full access to their own namespace:
  - Role namespace-admin (full rights within namespace) in each of dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
  - RoleBindings:
    - dev-alice/alice-full-access -> subjects: user alice and SA alice-sa
    - dev-bob/bob-full-access -> subjects: user bob and SA bob-sa
    - dev-charlie/charlie-full-access -> subjects: user charlie and SA charlie-sa
- Read-only access to dev-shared for all developers and their SAs:
  - Role dev-shared/shared-readonly with verbs: get, list, watch
  - RoleBinding dev-shared/developers-readonly -> subjects: users alice, bob, charlie and SAs alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa
- Staging/prod: no developer bindings created. Only users with cluster-admin (e.g., system:masters) can access.

Resource Quotas
- dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie (quota-standard):
  - requests.cpu: 2, limits.cpu: 2
  - requests.memory: 4Gi, limits.memory: 4Gi
  - pods: 10, services: 5
- dev-shared (quota-shared):
  - requests.cpu: 4, limits.cpu: 4
  - requests.memory: 8Gi, limits.memory: 8Gi
  - pods: 20, services: 10
- staging, prod (quota-env):
  - requests.cpu: 8, limits.cpu: 8
  - requests.memory: 16Gi, limits.memory: 16Gi
  - pods: 50, services: 20

Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress and Egress) applied to: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Same-namespace allow (Ingress and Egress) in each namespace above to permit intra-namespace communication
- Allow egress to kube-system in all non-system namespaces (to enable DNS and system service access)
- Developer egress scoped:
  - dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie: allow egress to dev-shared
- dev-shared ingress from dev namespaces only:
  - Allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces using namespaceSelector matching env=dev and purpose=developer
- Cross-developer isolation:
  - No policies allow cross-dev namespace communications; default deny plus lack of ingress rules enforce this
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - Only same-namespace traffic allowed; no ingress from dev namespaces; egress only to kube-system for DNS

Notes and validation
- Ensure your authentication setup maps users alice, bob, charlie as Kubernetes users (e.g., via client cert CNs or OIDC). The RBAC above expects those identities.
- Quick validation commands:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'            -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods         -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared create pods      -> no
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods            -> no
  - kubectl -n dev-alice run -it tmp --image=busybox -- rm -rf /          -> should schedule but be constrained by quota
  - From a pod in dev-alice, curl a service in dev-bob                    -> blocked
  - From a pod in dev-alice, nslookup kubernetes.default.svc              -> succeeds (DNS via kube-system)
  - From a pod in dev-alice, reach a service in dev-shared                -> allowed
  - From a pod in dev-alice, reach a service in staging/prod              -> blocked

If you want explicit RoleBindings in staging/prod for your admin group, bind ClusterRole cluster-admin to your admin group (e.g., system:masters) in those namespaces, although it’s typically unnecessary since cluster-admin is cluster-scoped.

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I set up:
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (for stable network IDs)
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - Container: busybox
  - Each pod mounts a 1Gi PVC at /data (volumeClaimTemplates: data)
  - Init container writes /data/test with initial_data on first start

Verification performed:
- Confirmed /data/test content is initial_data in db-0 and db-1.
- Then scaled the StatefulSet back down to 1 replica. Current running pod: db-0.

Notes and handy commands:
- Check the file content: kubectl -n statefulset-test exec db-0 -- sh -c 'cat -A /data/test'
- Scale up again (optional): kubectl -n statefulset-test scale statefulset db --replicas=3
- PVCs created per pod (retained on scale-down): data-db-0, data-db-1, data-db-2

    managedFields:
    - apiVersion: apps/v1
      fieldsType: FieldsV1
      fieldsV1:
        f:spec:

          f:template:
            f:metadata:

            f:spec:

                k:{"name":"app"}:
                  .: {}
                  f:command: {}
                  f:image: {}
                  f:name: {}
                  f:volumeMounts:
                    k:{"mountPath":"/data"}:
                      .: {}
                      f:mountPath: {}
                      f:name: {}
              f:initContainers:
                k:{"name":"init-data"}:
                  .: {}
                  f:command: {}
                  f:image: {}
                  f:name: {}
                  f:volumeMounts:
                    k:{"mountPath":"/data"}:
                      .: {}
                      f:mountPath: {}
                      f:name: {}
          f:updateStrategy:
            f:type: {}
          f:volumeClaimTemplates: {}
      manager: kubernetes-mcp-server
      operation: Apply
      time: "2026-07-06T11:30:13Z"
    name: db
    namespace: statefulset-test
    resourceVersion: "7920"
    uid: 5ebbbff6-2a89-4b9d-b173-a0afb31c9cec
  spec:
    persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy:
      whenDeleted: Retain
      whenScaled: Retain
    podManagementPolicy: OrderedReady

    selector:

    template:

        containers:
        - command:
          - sh
          - -c
          - while true; do sleep 3600; done
          image: busybox:1.36
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          name: app

          terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
          terminationMessagePolicy: File
          volumeMounts:
          - mountPath: /data

        dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
        initContainers:
        - command:
          - sh
          - -c

Steps.message:
Done.

What I set up:
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (for stable network IDs)
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - Container: busybox
  - Each pod mounts a 1Gi PVC at /data (volumeClaimTemplates: data)
  - Init container writes /data/test with initial_data on first start

Verification performed:
- Confirmed /data/test content is initial_data in db-0 and db-1.
- Then scaled the StatefulSet back down to 1 replica. Current running pod: db-0.

Notes and handy commands:
- Check the file content: kubectl -n statefulset-test exec db-0 -- sh -c 'cat -A /data/test'
- Scale up again (optional): kubectl -n statefulset-test scale statefulset db --replicas=3
- PVCs created per pod (retained on scale-down): data-db-0, data-db-1, data-db-2

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I set up:
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (for stable network IDs)
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - Container: busybox
  - Each pod mounts a 1Gi PVC at /data (volumeClaimTemplates: data)
  - Init container writes /data/test with initial_data on first start

Verification performed:
- Confirmed /data/test content is initial_data in db-0 and db-1.
- Then scaled the StatefulSet back down to 1 replica. Current running pod: db-0.

Notes and handy commands:
- Check the file content: kubectl -n statefulset-test exec db-0 -- sh -c 'cat -A /data/test'
- Scale up again (optional): kubectl -n statefulset-test scale statefulset db --replicas=3
- PVCs created per pod (retained on scale-down): data-db-0, data-db-1, data-db-2
